target_actor: Office of the Dean for Research
occurrence_sentence: Collaboration is the key to the quantum future and Princeton researchers are building partnerships across labs, across the university and around the world through foundational research in their labs and through collaborations with industry partners, according to the Office of the Dean for Research.

source_actor: Office of the Dean for Research
occurrence_sentence: Collaboration is the key to the quantum future and Princeton researchers are building partnerships across labs, across the university and around the world through foundational research in their labs and through collaborations with industry partners, according to the Office of the Dean for Research.
"What's exciting about Princeton is that we have real expertise in both the fundamental science and the engineering," said Andrew Houck, professor of electrical engineering, said in the story.
The initiative's inaugural director is Andrew Houck, professor of electrical engineering.

Princeton recently announced the creation of the [Princeton Quantum Initiative](https://www.princeton.edu/news/2019/09/25/princeton-announces-initiative-propel-innovations-quantum-science-and-technology) to foster exploration and education across the spectrum from fundamental quantum research to applications in areas such as computing, sensors and communications.
The initiative's inaugural director is Andrew Houck, professor of electrical engineering.
To address this challenge, Houck and his team are collaborating with IBM Research on an IARPA-funded project to fortify the transmon by building more complex circuits that guard against decoherence.
Jeffrey Thompson, assistant professor of electrical engineering, cools atoms down to incredibly low temperatures and traps them in a vacuum chamber.
Stephen Lyon, professor of electrical engineering, is one of the researchers exploring ways to keep spin qubits in superposition for relatively long periods.
